skill.name: context-fundamentals
skill.description: Foundational guidance for understanding, designing, debugging, and optimizing context in AI agent systems, including context windows, attention limits, progressive disclosure, and context budgeting.
skill.summary: Understand context engineering fundamentals for AI agents and long-running assistant workflows.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and most weak-cryptography detections are false positives caused by markdown fences, prose examples, and educational terminology. The bundled Python utility does perform local filesystem reads from caller-provided paths, so the skill is publishable with a medium filesystem warning and no evidence of malicious intent, network exfiltration, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted Local File Loading Utility
medium_findings.description: ProgressiveDisclosureManager accepts summary_path and detail_path values and reads them directly with open(). This is legitimate for a local context-management helper, but a host application must not pass untrusted paths because the base_dir value is not enforced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads are explicit and the paths come from caller-provided reference data. There is no network sink or malicious intent, so the concern is contextual rather than a confirmed exploit.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby and shell backtick detections occur in fenced markdown examples and prose. No executable shell call, Ruby backtick execution, subprocess invocation, or command string construction was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are documentation examples inside markdown fences or plain instructional text. They are not executed by the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Mostly Non-Security Context
low_findings.description: Most weak-cryptography alerts point to prose about context engineering rather than cryptographic code. The only concrete hash use is hashlib.md5 for duplicate-content fingerprints, not authentication, encryption, or security integrity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The markdown locations do not implement cryptography, and the Python MD5 use is limited to non-security duplicate detection. It could be replaced with SHA-256 for hygiene, but it is not evidence of malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts refer to documentation about metadata, stored observations, and context limits. No code collects host details, environment variables, secrets, usernames, process lists, or network configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explanatory text and pseudocode patterns for context management. I did not find evidence of host reconnaissance behavior in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled File Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python helper reads files from paths supplied by the caller without normalizing or checking them against base_dir. Use only with trusted paths or add path allowlisting in applications that expose this helper to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in load_summary and load_detail. The impact depends on how an integrating application passes paths to the utility.

content.value_statement: Context-heavy AI agents can become slow, expensive, and unreliable when teams overload the model window. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical framework for context budgets, progressive disclosure, and agent architecture reviews.
actual_capabilities: Explains the main components of model context, including prompts, tools, documents, history, and tool outputs.
Describes how context windows, attention mechanics, and position effects influence agent behavior.
Provides practical guidance for progressive disclosure and just-in-time document loading.
Gives examples for organizing system prompts and reference material.
Includes a Python utility for estimating token usage, truncating messages, and building context sections.
Helps review context-related architecture choices for AI agent systems.
limitations: Token counting uses rough character and word estimates, not provider-specific tokenizers.
The Python helper is demonstrative and should be hardened before use with untrusted file paths.
It teaches concepts and patterns; it does not integrate with a specific model provider API.
It does not benchmark model performance or automatically optimize production prompts.

use_cases.title: Design an Agent Context Plan
use_cases.description: Map which information belongs in system prompts, tools, retrieved documents, message history, and tool outputs.

use_cases.title: Debug Context-Related Failures
use_cases.description: Reason about missing information, overloaded context, poor ordering, or excessive tool output during agent tasks.

use_cases.title: Teach Context Engineering Basics
use_cases.description: Use the reference material to onboard teammates to context windows, attention budgets, and progressive disclosure.

prompt_templates.title: Explain My Context Problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain why my AI agent may be losing track of instructions during long tasks. Use context engineering terms and keep the explanation practical.

prompt_templates.title: Review Context Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent design and identify what should live in the system prompt, tool definitions, retrieved documents, message history, and tool outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Context Budget
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a context budget for this agent workflow. Include token allocation, compaction triggers, and what to load only on demand.

prompt_templates.title: Redesign for Progressive Disclosure
prompt_templates.prompt: Redesign this context strategy using progressive disclosure. Separate startup context from just-in-time references and explain the tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: My support agent loads all policy documents at startup and gets slower during long tickets.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend a smaller startup prompt, policy summaries, targeted retrieval for the active issue, and compaction before history dominates the context.

output_examples.input: I need to teach engineers why larger context windows do not solve every memory problem.
output_examples.output: The skill would produce a concise explanation of attention budget limits, information placement, context quality, and progressive disclosure.

output_examples.input: Our agent repeats old tool output and misses the newest instructions.
output_examples.output: The skill would examine message history size, tool output verbosity, ordering effects, and whether summaries should replace older turns.

best_practices: Keep startup context small and load detailed references only when they are needed.
Place critical instructions where the model is most likely to attend to them.
Measure context usage during development and define compaction thresholds before production use.
anti_patterns: Loading every document into context before the task requires it.
Treating a larger context window as a replacement for context design.
Passing verbose tool outputs forward when a summary or reference would be enough.

faq.question: What is context engineering?
faq.answer: Context engineering is the practice of selecting and organizing the information a model receives for a task.

faq.question: Does this skill run model evaluations?
faq.answer: No. It provides concepts, patterns, examples, and a small local utility for context management experiments.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance applies to assistant and agent workflows that depend on model context.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files do not make network requests or contact external services.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The bundled Python helper can read caller-provided local file paths, so integrations should restrict paths when users control inputs.

faq.question: Is the static command-execution warning confirmed?
faq.answer: No. The command warnings are markdown fence false positives, not executable command calls.
